speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is July 21, 1995. We are in the home of Mr. Herman Haller, H-A-L-L-E-R. The interview is being conducted by Henry Korobelnik, K-O-R-O-B-E-L-N-I-K. It is being conducted in Mr. Haller's home at 76-27 167th Street in Flushing, New York, 11365, in the United States of America. And the interview will be conducted in English. Could you please state your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Herman Haller, H-A-L-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Haller, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was July 6, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Anna, and my father's name was Victor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have or do you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother, Alfred. He is three years younger than myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of our education did you have as a child or a young adult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Berlin, only elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went-- first we went to regular public school, and then later we went to a Jewish school from the Adas Israel Congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of education did that afford you in the Adas school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a secular education and religious education both.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to that school up until what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about 14 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from a religious background or religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a religious orthodox home. We observed the holidays. The store was closed on the Shabbat. We had a kosher home, and we observed all the commandments.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work was your family engaged in at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my family was in the furniture business. We had a furniture store in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you say something or share something with us in terms of what life was like pre-war in Berlin in terms of your home and your family, the kinds of things that you did, et cetera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, like I said, first we went to a secular school, a public school. I was-- I was about nine years old when the Nazis regime came to power in Germany. So right from the beginning, the public school became very uncomfortable for us because of the anti-Semitism. So we had to-- we had to go that to Jewish school, and I vividly remember the boycott of the Nazis in 1933. And in April of 1933, I remember very vividly how the Nazis in their brown uniforms and their armbands with their swastikas were standing in front of our stores, and they had signs-- you know, what do you call these, these-- signs where they said do not buy by Jews. And they also said that, die Juden sind unser Ungluck. They said that 'the Jews are our misfortune.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very difficult to conduct business that way but somehow-- we managed somehow, difficult as it was. And then, of course, in November-- November 9, 1938, that was the Kristallnacht. [CLEARS THROAT] All of the Jewish businesses were destroyed. Our store was vandalized and all the windows were open, some of the furnitures were carried away and all the synagogues were put on fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was-- I was only 14 years old, and my mother was very worried about her sister, Frances. See, her sister also had a furniture store, and she had two little children at that time. They were very small. And we knew that where she lived there was a synagogue in the back of the building. There was another building, and that building was a synagogue, and we were trying to get in touch with her. We couldn't. Somehow, we couldn't get in touch with her. There was nobody home. So finally, the next morning my mother said that I should take my bicycle and go and see what had happened to her. So I did this, and as they went out, I went through the neighborhood there. And I saw, of course, all the Jewish shops were all broken into, all the glass was laying in the street. As a matter of fact, on our street where our store was, there was a synagogue which was on a first floor of another building. And that synagogue, they could not burn it because there were people living in the building. So what they did, they throw all the Torah scrolls out in the street. Everything was lay in the street, the benches, the books. And it was totally destroyed. But I just took my bicycle, and I kept on going to my aunt's house, which is about 10 minutes away. And when I arrived there, I saw a large crowd standing in front of the building there. And the fire department was there and the police department was there. And what they did, they were-- the fire department poured water onto the adjacent building because the synagogue was on fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they poured that water not on the synagogue to put out the fire, but they put it onto the adjacent building so the adjacent building would not burn. And then as I mingled with the crowd, I found out that the people that are living there, they were all evacuated. And then I found out that my aunt was safe, that she was somewhere in the neighborhood with other friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back right away. I went back to my mother because I didn't want to stay there, because I didn't want to arouse attraction. I was afraid myself, so I peddled back to our home, and I reported to my mother what had happened. Then after that we could not-- of course, the store we couldn't re-open. We had to keep the store closed, and there was no store, as a matter of fact. It was totally destroyed. And the Nazi regime, on top of all the damages that they did, they imposed a fine on the Jewish population of one billion marks. In other words, they said that we had to pay for the damages that they did. So it was impossible to stay in Berlin. We had to get out, and the only way to do it now was my mother had a brother that was living in Belgium. He was not really living there. He was a transient because he originally lived in Berlin. And he also had a furniture store, but he managed to get a Visa to come to the United States. And that just happened just a few months before this incident of the Kristallnacht. So what he did, he was smart. He did not want to stay in Berlin and wait for his passage to go to America. He went to Antwerp, to Belgium, to wait over there to get passage on a boat. Because in those days, there were no planes. You couldn't go to the airport and buy a ticket to fly to America, you had to book a passage on a ship. And those ships didn't go that frequently, so instead of waiting in Germany, he waited out his turn in Antwerp. So my mother got in touch with her brother and told her what had happened and that he has to help us to get out of Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they devised a plan that they would take-- there was a train that left every night from Berlin, from the Hauptbahnhof in Berlin, that went from Berlin to Paris with a stop over in Brussels. And my mother went ahead and she bought a ticket for myself and for my brother, a return ticket to Paris and back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were supposed to take this train and go to Brussels. When the train stopped in Brussels, my uncle from Belgium would wait for us on the platform and would take us off the train. Of course, we never intended to go to Paris, that was just to show that we were going to Paris. As it turned out it was a very good scheme, because when we got to the border and the-- the border patrol came, the passport control came on the train to ask for passports, we, of course, had no passports and we had no Visas to go. And you have to remember, though, that we were both two young children. I was 14, my brother was 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the passport control asked us for our Visas, we just showed them our ticket to Paris, return ticket to Paris, and we showed them our identity cards which was marked with a large red J to indicate that we were Jews. So the border patrol looked at those papers, there were two of them. And one said to the other, well, they're going to Paris. Let the French worry about them. So that's how we go through the border. We got into Brussels, my uncle was waiting there with my aunt at the station, and they took us off the train, and we went with him to Antwerp where he was living in a rooming house. And the lady at-- at the rooming house had another room available upstairs underneath the roof, like what they call a [NON-ENGLISH], they called it over there. It's a room right under the roof. And they gave that to us and we stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my mother, of course, she could not do this because a grown up, an adult, could never pass that scheme, but we did. So she was supposed to go to Aachen. Aachen is the last town from Germany to Belgium on the border. That's right on the boarder of Belgium. And there she was to meet the guide they would take her and some of the people during the night over the border. They paid him some money for that and that's how they were supposed to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when they got there they were caught by the German border patrol, and they were sent back to Berlin. So now we were in Belgium and my mother was back in Berlin, so we had to make a big decision. Should we go back to Berlin, or should we stay and wait for another occasion that my mother could go again with another guide to get over the border? So we decided that they should stay because once we got out of Berlin, it would be best to stay, to stay in Belgium, and not to go back. But my mother could not go again to the-- to Aachen, to the border, because after that incidence, the border was tied up very tight. They made it very difficult to cross the border illegally. Because many people did this before. There were many people that managed to get into Belgian that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after that, they were not able to do this. So my mother was stuck in Berlin, we were in Belgium, my uncle had this Visa to go to America where his ship was coming in. And any day in the next few weeks, he had to leave. So we were really desperate, and we didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, they found out we had a distant relative that lived in Paris. He was a French citizen. He lived in Paris all his life. So we got in contact with him. And he said, OK, send to children to me. We will try to take care of them. So what they did, he came-- this relative, his name was Emmanuel. He came to the Belgian border, and he met the train on the Belgium side. We were on the train. And he said to us, when the border patrol comes, you don't say nothing, let me do all the talking. So they ask him for his papers, and he, of course, had a French passport. He showed his papers. And they ask him, who are the two children? So he said, those are my children. So they didn't bother us, and we went into France. So now we were near Paris in Brunoy, he had a little house in Brunoy, that's near Paris. Now my mother, again, she was still in Berlin now. In the meantime, the uncle from Brussels, from Belgium, from Antwerp, his ship came in and he had to leave for New York. And my mother was still stuck in Berlin. And she could not-- there was no way for her to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, she got a paper in January of 1939-- she got a paper from the police precinct from Berlin to tell her that she had to leave the territory of the Reich within eight weeks, and if she doesn't leave then she will be subject to imprisonment and heavy fines. And they said that this order can be repealed, but it also said that in the public interest, this appeal will be denied. So it's a catch-22. And of course she was desperate and she found some-- through a relative, she found out that in England you could go to England if you would work as a domestic in a household. Because in England, there was a shortage of domestic help. And if you could get a job, if you could show that you had a job there, they would give you a permit to go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through friends and relatives, she managed to get such a permit. And because she was working on this permit, they extended her stay in Berlin until she was able to get out. So this way, she was able to go to England and work as a domestic in a household in London-- near London. In Croyden, it was. This was outside London.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, we were still in France, my mother was now in England, and the uncle was in New York. So the family was really cut up. So the situation in Paris, though, got also very uncomfortable because the French police was not as lenient to us, the refugees, as the Belgium police was. When they caught somebody, they would send them back to the German border and send them back to Germany. And that, of course, meant to go to a concentration camp. So this relative, this Emmanuel, he was very frightened because we could not speak French. We could not go outside and play. He had a little garden in his house, and that's the only place where we could go out. He would not let this out in the street because he was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, one day, he said, look, it would be better for you to go back to Belgium, because in Belgium you have a better chance to get help and the police will not bother you, they will not send you back to Germany. So he helped us, he brought us back to the Belgian border and brought us back over the border the same way as he did previously. So we went to Antwerp, we arrived in Antwerp. And in Antwerp, there were many refugees that had escaped from Germany. And there was a committee, a Jewish committee, which got help from America from the Joint Distribution Committee. They had set up a kitchen where people would come every day and eat. And we went to them, and we told them our situation. So since we were two young children, they wouldn't let us live by ourselves. So they put us up in a home, in homes with people, with Jewish people that live-- Belgian people, Belgian Jewish people. And there were other children that were in the same predicament that we were in. So some of the children were fortunate, they came into a home that was a nice home where there were well-to-do people, and they did it for-- for doing a good deed, to helping other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were not that fortunate. We got into a home that they did it for the money, because they got paid for taking care of us. The Joint Distribution paid these people every month, they paid them. I don't remember how much, but I guess not too much money. But these people needed that money, and they did it, like I said, for the money. They were so poor themselves that I remember that like, we used to get one egg per week. They used to boil this egg in hot water. And then instead of throwing out the water, they would take that same hot water and make tea for us. That's how poor with these people were. That was before the war, mind you. That it was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were not very comfortable there, but we had no choice. Of course, we could corresponded with our mother in England. I forgot to mention, though, that my mother was divorced. My father-- my father and my mother did not live together. They were divorced for many years already. They divorced before-- they divorce in 1930 already. We were very young then, and my father had managed-- he had a relative in Palestine then, in Israel, which was then Palestine. And he had gone to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how my mother was alone by herself. But what this committee in Belgium, what they were trying to do was they wanted to make an exchange with some children that were in England which were in a similar situation like we were. Because they were children in England where the parents were in Belgium. Because the situation in Germany was so desperate that people used to send their children away wherever they could. And they followed-- some of them followed later and others didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were children in England where their parents were in Belgium, and we were in Belgium where the parent was in England. So they approach the English government to make an exchange, to send two children from England to Belgium, and they would send two children from Belgium to England. But the English government would not cooperate, they would not allow this. So we were stuck till-- till May, 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the war broke out in Belgium. Belgium was occupied by the Nazis. Within 10 days, they occupy it all of Holland, Belgium, and France. And the people that we stayed with, they had gone away. When the German advanced, many people-- many Belgian people started to go south. They went to France because they had thought that they would not get there to France. But unfortunately, the army, the German army was so fast, they were so mobilized, that they overrun these people, and they overrun them before they ever got there. So eventually, they all came back. But while these people were away, we opened up a room also on the roof, under the roof, another room that belonged to them which they had rented to a young man, incidentally. And this young man also went away-- also went away to France. And he did not come back right away, so we stayed in that room all by ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course it was very difficult for us, because we had no food. We had no money to buy food, so we actually had to beg for food. And then everything got rationed. Once the occupation started, they put out ration cards. And we ourselves, my brother and myself, we could not get any ration cards. We applied for them but they would not give to us because they said, hey, how can two young children live by themselves? They're-- they're trying to cheat the government, they're trying to get extra cards for their families. So they would not give us a ration cards, and we couldn't even buy the meager rations that they were giving out. So we were in a very desperate situation there. And on top of it, the people that owned the place, the people that we were staying with came back, and they wanted their place back and they throw us, virtually throw us, out in the street because we had no money to pay them. The joint wasn't there anymore because the joint was sending money from America. And the war was going on, so they couldn't send any more money, so we were on our own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since my brother was only at that time 12 years old, he-- another family had pity on us, and they took him in. A family of better means who could afford to do this without being paid. And they had no children, so they took him in, and they took care of him. And I myself, I had to look for odd jobs. I was not allowed-- officially, I was not allowed to work because the Belgium authority let us stay there as refugees. They gave us what they call a permit that was good for three months. But with that permit, you were not allowed to work, and every three months you had to go back to the police, you had to register, and they would renew that permit for another three months. So they did not really bother you, they would not send you back to Germany, but they kept watching you. But I found some odd jobs. I found a job in a bakery, which was very good because at least I had bread to eat. This particular bakery, for instance, before the war, this was a very fancy bakery. They used to make cakes and they had a coffee shop. But all this was gone now because during the war, they were only allowed to bake bread. If they were caught to bake any other goods like rolls or challah or anything like that, that would be subject to heavy fines and maybe even closed up altogether. So we were only allowed to buy the official bread that was allowed to be sold with those food rations. But it was a warm place, in the bakery it was warm. And I used to sleep in the bakery right on the benches where we used to bake the bread. I used to sleep there. And this went on for awhile, and then two friends that appealed to the authorities-- we were finally able to get those ration cards. Which was, then, a big help because at least we were able to buy the meager rations that they were giving out. And after a while, in 19-- this was still in 1940, 41. And in May of 1942, they started to-- already in the beginning of 1942, they started to impose the same laws against the Jews that they did in Germany. You had to wear a Jewish star, a yellow star. And you had to change your names. You had to-- for instance, my name was Herman Haller, I had to be called Abraham. All men, all males had to be called Abraham, and all females had to be called Sarah. They had to add that to their name. Like my mother, for instance, her name was Anna. So her name was Anna Sarah Haller. And so they imposed all these so-called Nuremberg Laws. They called them Nuremberg Laws because these laws were initiated from the town of Nuremberg in Germany.
